title: Christmas Eve

infobox.value: Vigil of the Nativity Christmas Evening Christmas Vigil Day before Christmas Night before Christmas
Day or evening preceding the traditional birthday of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity")

Gift shopping, gift giving, [goodwill greetings](/wiki/Christmas_and_holiday_season#Greetings "Christmas and holiday season"), Midnight [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)"), other [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service"), meals, preparations for the arrival of [Christmas gift-bringers](/wiki/Christmas_gift-bringer "Christmas gift-bringer"), preparing for [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas")

* 24 December ([Western Churches](/wiki/Western_Churches "Western Churches") and [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") churches that use the Revised Julian Calendar) * 5 January ([Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church")) * 6 January ([Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") Churches that follow the Old Julian Calendar and most [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox "Oriental Orthodox") Churches) * 18 January ([Armenian Patriarchate of Jerusalem](/wiki/Armenian_Patriarchate_of_Jerusalem "Armenian Patriarchate of Jerusalem"))[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide"), [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve"), [New Year's Day](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Day "New Year's Day")

is the evening or entire day before [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), the festival commemorating [the birth](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ").[[4]](#cite_note-Christmas_Eve-4) Christmas Eve is widely recognized as a full or partial holiday in anticipation of Christmas Day, which is [observed around the world](/wiki/Observance_of_Christmas_by_country "Observance of Christmas by country"). Together, both days are considered one of the most culturally significant celebrations in [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") and [Western society](/wiki/Western_world "Western world").

**Christmas Eve** is the evening or entire day before [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), the festival commemorating [the birth](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ").[[4]](#cite_note-Christmas_Eve-4) Christmas Eve is widely recognized as a full or partial holiday in anticipation of Christmas Day, which is [observed around the world](/wiki/Observance_of_Christmas_by_country "Observance of Christmas by country"). Together, both days are considered one of the most culturally significant celebrations in [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") and [Western society](/wiki/Western_world "Western world").

Christmas celebrations in the [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") of [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") have long begun on Christmas Eve, due in part to the Christian liturgical day starting at sunset,[[5]](#cite_note-Jerusalem_Center-5) a practice inherited from Jewish tradition,[[6]](#cite_note-Dictionary-6) and based on the [story of Creation](/wiki/Genesis_creation_narrative "Genesis creation narrative") in the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis"): "And there was evening, and there was morning—the first day."[[7]](#cite_note-Genesis-7) Many churches still ring their [church bells](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") and hold [prayers](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") in the evening; for example, the Nordic [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") churches.[[8]](#cite_note-NE-8) Since tradition holds that [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") was born at night (based in Luke 2:6-8), [Midnight Mass](/wiki/Midnight_Mass "Midnight Mass") is celebrated on Christmas Eve, traditionally at midnight, in commemoration of his birth.[[9]](#cite_note-Vatican_Today-9) The idea of Jesus being born at night is reflected in the fact that Christmas Eve is referred to as *Heilige Nacht* (Holy Night) in German, *Nochebuena* (the Good Night) in Spanish and similarly in other expressions of Christmas spirituality, such as the song ["Silent Night, Holy Night"](/wiki/Silent_Night "Silent Night").

Many other varying cultural [traditions](/wiki/Tradition "Tradition") and experiences are also associated with Christmas Eve around the world, including the gathering of family and friends, the singing of [Christmas carols](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol"), the illumination and enjoyment of [Christmas lights](/wiki/Christmas_light "Christmas light"), trees, and other decorations, the wrapping, exchange and opening of gifts, and general preparation for Christmas Day. Legendary Christmas gift-bearing figures including [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus"), [Father Christmas](/wiki/Father_Christmas "Father Christmas"), [Christkind](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind"), and [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas") are also often said to depart for their annual journey to deliver presents to children around the world on Christmas Eve, although until the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") introduction of Christkind in 16th-century Europe,[[10]](#cite_note-10) such figures were said to instead deliver presents on the eve of [Saint Nicholas' feast day](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas_Day "Saint Nicholas Day") (6 December).

## Religious traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Religious traditions")]

### Western churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Western churches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Midnight_Mass_2010_-_66_%285292245955%29.jpg/250px-Midnight_Mass_2010_-_66_%285292245955%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Midnight_Mass_2010_-_66_(5292245955).jpg)

[Midnight Mass](/wiki/Midnight_Mass "Midnight Mass") is held in many churches toward the end of Christmas Eve, often with dim lighting and traditional decorative accents such as greenery.

Western churches have traditionally observed Christmas Eve (properly the Vigil of the Nativity) as a liturgical observance distinct from the masses of Christmas Day, with the proper Gospel at the Mass for the Vigil of the Nativity being that of the Annunciation to Joseph in [Matthew 1](/wiki/Matthew_1 "Matthew 1"). The Vigil of the Nativity is not so much the first day of Christmas as it is the last day of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"), and so it traditionally retains the [liturgical color](/wiki/Liturgical_colours "Liturgical colours") of violet. In traditional western liturgical practice, when the Fourth Sunday of Advent and Christmas Eve occur on the same day, the Sunday mass is of Christmas Eve and the Fourth Sunday of Advent is only commemorated.

The festivities of Christmas Day have, however, extended farther and farther back into Christmas Eve. While [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans"), and some [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") traditionally celebrate [Midnight Mass](/wiki/Midnight_Mass "Midnight Mass"), the first mass of Christmas, either at or near [midnight](/wiki/Midnight "Midnight") on Christmas Eve, some churches have in recent decades scheduled their [Midnight Mass](/wiki/Midnight_Mass "Midnight Mass") as early as 7 pm in an effort to better accommodate young children, whose choral singing has become a popular feature in some traditions.

Midnight Mass is held in churches throughout the world and celebrates the birth of Christ, which is believed to have occurred at night. Midnight Mass is popular in Poland ([pasterka](/wiki/Pasterka "Pasterka")) and Lithuania (piemenėlių mišios). In [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), the Midnight Mass is also referred to as "Rooster's Mass" (*[Misa de Gallo](/wiki/Misa_de_Gallo "Misa de Gallo")* in Spanish, *Missa do Galo* in Portuguese and *Missa del Gall* in Catalan). In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), the custom has expanded into the nine-day *[Simbang Gabi](/wiki/Simbang_Gabi "Simbang Gabi")*, when Filipinos attend dawn Masses (traditionally beginning around 4 to 5 am [PST](/wiki/Philippine_Standard_Time "Philippine Standard Time")) from 16 December on until Christmas Eve. In 2009 [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") officials scheduled the Midnight Mass to start at 10 pm so that the 82-year-old [Pope Benedict XVI](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XVI "Pope Benedict XVI") would not have too late a night.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

A [nativity scene](/wiki/Nativity_scene "Nativity scene") may be erected indoors or outdoors, and is composed of figurines depicting the infant Jesus resting in a manger, Mary, and Joseph.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Other figures in the scene may include [angels](/wiki/Angel "Angel"), [shepherds](/wiki/Shepherd "Shepherd"), and various animals. The figures may be made of any material,[[13]](#cite_note-Dues-13) and arranged in a stable or grotto. The [Magi](/wiki/Magi "Magi") may also appear, and are sometimes not placed in the scene until the week following Christmas to account for their travel time to [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"). While most home nativity scenes are packed away at Christmas or shortly thereafter, nativity scenes in churches usually remain on display until the feast of the [Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Baptism_of_the_Lord "Baptism of the Lord").[[13]](#cite_note-Dues-13)

Throughout congregations of the various denominations of Christianity, on Christmas Eve, the Christ Candle in the center of the [Advent wreath](/wiki/Advent_wreath "Advent wreath") is traditionally lit in many [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service"). In candlelight services, while singing *[Silent Night](/wiki/Silent_Night "Silent Night")*, each member of the congregation receives a candle and passes along their flame which is first received from the Christ Candle.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Advent2007candlelight.JPG/250px-Advent2007candlelight.JPG)](/wiki/File:Advent2007candlelight.JPG)

Advent wreath, lighting the candle

[Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") traditionally practice Christmas Eve [Eucharistic](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") traditions typical of Germany and Scandinavia. "Krippenspiele" ([Nativity plays](/wiki/Nativity_play "Nativity play")), special festive music for organ, vocal and brass [choirs](/wiki/Choir "Choir") and candlelight services make Christmas Eve one of the most beloved days in the Lutheran Church calendar. Christmas [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") is popular in the early evening, and [Midnight Masses](/wiki/Midnight_Mass "Midnight Mass") are also widespread in regions which are predominantly Lutheran. The old Lutheran tradition of a Christmas Vigil in the early morning hours of [Christmas Day](/wiki/Christmas_Day "Christmas Day") (Christmette) can still be found in some regions. In eastern and [middle Germany](/wiki/Central_Germany_(geography) "Central Germany (geography)"), congregations still continue the tradition of "[Quempas](/wiki/Quempas "Quempas") singing": separate groups dispersed in various parts of the church sing verses of the song "He whom shepherds once came Praising" (*Quem pastores laudavere*) responsively.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Presepe_naples_rome2.jpg/250px-Presepe_naples_rome2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presepe_naples_rome2.jpg)

Typical [Neapolitan nativity scene](/wiki/Neapolitan_nativity_scene "Neapolitan nativity scene"), or *presepe* or *presepio*, in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").

With respect to [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland") has a service beginning just before midnight, in which [carols](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol") are sung. These services are very popular.

[Methodists](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") celebrate the evening in different ways. Some, in the early evening, come to their church to celebrate [Holy Communion](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist") with their families. The mood is very solemn, and the only visible light is the [Advent Wreath](/wiki/Advent_Wreath "Advent Wreath"), and the candles upon the [Lord's Table](/wiki/Lord%27s_Table "Lord's Table"). Others celebrate the evening with services of light, which include singing the song *Silent Night* as a variety of candles (including personal candles) are lit. Other churches have late evening services perhaps at 11 pm, so that the church can celebrate Christmas Day together with the ringing of bells at midnight. Others offer Christmas Day services as well.

The annual "[Nine Lessons and Carols](/wiki/Nine_Lessons_and_Carols "Nine Lessons and Carols")", broadcast from [King's College, Cambridge](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Cambridge "King's College, Cambridge") on Christmas Eve, has established itself a Christmas custom in the United Kingdom, where Anglican Christianity is the traditional faith.[[14]](#cite_note-nine-14) It is broadcast outside the UK via the [BBC World Service](/wiki/BBC_World_Service "BBC World Service"), and is also bought by broadcasters around the world.[[14]](#cite_note-nine-14)

### Eastern churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Eastern churches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/2013-12-24--Artoklasia_during_Christmas_Eve_service.JPG/250px-2013-12-24--Artoklasia_during_Christmas_Eve_service.JPG)](/wiki/File:2013-12-24--Artoklasia_during_Christmas_Eve_service.JPG)

Annunciation of the Virgin Mary Greek Orthodox Cathedral, Toronto, Ontario, Canada

In the [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite"), Christmas Eve is referred to as **Paramony** ("preparation"). It is the concluding day of the [Nativity Fast](/wiki/Nativity_Fast "Nativity Fast") and is observed as a day of strict [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") by those devout Byzantine Christians who are physically capable of doing so. In some traditions, nothing is eaten until the first star appears in the evening sky, in commemoration of the [Star of Bethlehem](/wiki/Star_of_Bethlehem "Star of Bethlehem"). The [liturgical](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") celebration begins earlier in the day with the celebration of the [Royal Hours](/wiki/Royal_Hours "Royal Hours"), followed by the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") combined with the celebration of [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers"), during which a large number of passages from the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") are chanted, recounting the history of salvation. After the [dismissal](/wiki/Dismissal_(liturgy) "Dismissal (liturgy)") at the end of the service, a new candle is brought out into the center of the church and lit, and all gather round and sing the [Troparion](/wiki/Troparion "Troparion") and [Kontakion](/wiki/Kontakion "Kontakion") of the Feast.

In the evening, the [All-Night Vigil](/wiki/All-Night_Vigil "All-Night Vigil") for the Feast of the Nativity is composed of [Great Compline](/wiki/Great_Compline "Great Compline"), [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins") and the [First Hour](/wiki/First_Hour "First Hour"). The Byzantine services of Christmas Eve are intentionally parallel to those of [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), illustrating the [theological](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_theology "Eastern Orthodox theology") point that the purpose of the [Incarnation](/wiki/Incarnation_of_Christ "Incarnation of Christ") was to make possible the [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") and [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"). This is illustrated in Eastern [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of the Nativity, on which the [Christ Child](/wiki/Christ_Child "Christ Child") is wrapped in [swaddling clothes](/wiki/Swaddling_clothes "Swaddling clothes") reminiscent of his [burial wrappings](/wiki/Burial_shroud "Burial shroud"). The child is also shown lying on a stone, representing the [Tomb of Christ](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre "Holy Sepulchre"), rather than a [manger](/wiki/Manger "Manger"). The [Cave of the Nativity](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nativity "Church of the Nativity") is also a reminder of the cave in which Jesus was buried.

The services of Christmas Eve are also similar to those of the Eve of [Theophany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday)#Eastern_Orthodox_churches "Epiphany (holiday)") (Epiphany), and the two [Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts "Great Feasts") are considered one celebration.

In some [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") cultures, after the Vesperal Liturgy the family returns home to a festive meal, but one at which [Orthodox fasting rules](/wiki/Fasting#Eastern_Orthodoxy "Fasting") are still observed: no meat or dairy products (milk, cheese, eggs, etc.) are consumed (see below for variations according to nationality). Then they return to the church for the All-Night Vigil.

The next morning, Christmas Day, the Divine Liturgy is celebrated again, but with special features that occur only on Great Feasts of the Lord. After the [dismissal](/wiki/Dismissal_(liturgy) "Dismissal (liturgy)") of this Liturgy, the faithful customarily greet each other with the [kiss of peace](/wiki/Kiss_of_peace "Kiss of peace") and the words: "Christ is Born!", to which the one being greeted responds: "Glorify Him!" (the opening words of the [Canon](/wiki/Canon_(hymnography) "Canon (hymnography)") of the Nativity that was chanted the night before during the Vigil). This greeting, together with many of the [hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") of the feast, continue to be used until the [leave-taking](/wiki/Leave-taking "Leave-taking") of the feast on 31 December.

The first three days of the feast are particularly solemn. The second day is known as the [Synaxis](/wiki/Synaxis "Synaxis") of the [Theotokos](/wiki/Theotokos "Theotokos"), and commemorates the role of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") in the [Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus"). The third day is referred to simply as "the Third Day of the Nativity". The Saturday and Sunday following 25 December have special [Epistle](/wiki/Epistle "Epistle") and [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel "Gospel") readings assigned to them. 29 December celebrates the [Holy Innocents](/wiki/Holy_Innocents "Holy Innocents").

Byzantine Christians observe a festal period of [twelve days](/wiki/Twelve_Days_of_Christmas "Twelve Days of Christmas"), during which no one in the Church fasts, even on Wednesdays and Fridays, which are normal fasting days throughout the rest of the year. During this time one feast leads into another: 25–31 December is the [afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") of the Nativity; 2–5 January is the [forefeast](/wiki/Forefeast "Forefeast") of the [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)").

## Christmas Eve dinner

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Christmas Eve dinner")]

Further information: [List of Christmas dishes](/wiki/List_of_Christmas_dishes "List of Christmas dishes")

### Bulgaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bulgaria")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/7_Traditional_Christmas_Eve_dishes_2014.jpg/250px-7_Traditional_Christmas_Eve_dishes_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:7_Traditional_Christmas_Eve_dishes_2014.jpg)

Seven traditional Christmas Eve dishes from Bulgaria (2014)

In Bulgaria, the meal consists of an [odd number](/wiki/Odd_number "Odd number") of lenten dishes in compliance with the rules of fasting. They are usually the traditional [sarma](/wiki/Sarma_(food) "Sarma (food)"), [bob chorba](/wiki/Bob_chorba "Bob chorba") (bean soup), fortune [kravai](/wiki/Korovai "Korovai") (pastry with a fortune in it; also called *bogovitsa*, *vechernik*, *kolednik*), [stuffed peppers](/wiki/Stuffed_peppers "Stuffed peppers"), [nuts](/wiki/Nut_(fruit) "Nut (fruit)"), dried fruit, boiled wheat.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The meal is often accompanied with wine or Bulgaria's traditional [alcoholic beverage](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage") [rakia](/wiki/Rakia "Rakia"), in the past *olovina* (a type of homemade rye beer). The meals used to be put on top of hay, directly on the floor, together with a [ploughshare](/wiki/Ploughshare "Ploughshare") or a [coulter](/wiki/Coulter_(agriculture) "Coulter (agriculture)").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Cuba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Cuba")]

In Cuba, roasted pig (*[lechón](/wiki/Lechon "Lechon")*) is often the center of Christmas Eve (*Nochebuena*).[[17]](#cite_note-17) It is believed that the tradition dates back to the 15th century when Caribbean colonists hunted down pigs and roasted them with a powerful flame.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In Cuban and Cuban-American tradition, the pig is sometimes cooked in a *Caja China*, a large box where an entire pig is placed below hot coals.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The dinner features many side dishes and desserts, and often games of [dominoes](/wiki/Dominoes "Dominoes") are played. The tradition is continued by Cuban families in the United States.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The dinner on Christmas Eve is the center of the celebration.

Christmas Eve dinner is generally not served at a set time, though it is typically eaten as a family. It is expected that the entire family will be at the table to start tasting the *frijoles negros dormidos* (sleeping black beans) and the *arroz blanco desgranado y reluciente* (shredded white rice), the *yuca con mojo* (a Cuban side dish made by marinating [cassava](/wiki/Cassava "Cassava") in garlic, sour orange, and olive oil), the roasted pork or the stuffed or unfilled *guanajo*, along with homemade desserts, such as Christmas fritters, and a wide range of sweets in syrup and Spanish nougat.

The visit to the archipelago of [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") [John Paul II](/wiki/John_Paul_II "John Paul II"), in 1998, prompted the Cuban government, in a gesture of goodwill, to declare 25 December again as a holiday, which it had not been for several decades.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Czech Republic and Slovakia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Czech Republic and Slovakia")]

In the Czech Republic and Slovakia, fasting on the day of Christmas Eve (or only eating meatless food) is a medieval tradition. The belief is that if one lasted until Christmas dinner, they would see a golden pig, which is a symbol of luck. A typical Christmas breakfast is a sweet braided bread *[vánočka](/wiki/V%C3%A1no%C4%8Dka "Vánočka")*. Christmas Eve dinner traditionally consists of a carp (baked or fried) and a potato salad.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: France")]

In French-speaking places, [Réveillon](/wiki/R%C3%A9veillon "Réveillon") is a long dinner eaten on Christmas Eve.

### Guam and the Northern Marianas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Guam and the Northern Marianas")]

In [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam") and the [Northern Marianas](/wiki/Northern_Marianas "Northern Marianas"), dishes include shrimp *[kelaguen](/wiki/Kelaguen "Kelaguen")*; coconut crab; and *kadon* octopus (octopus stewed in sweet peppers and [coconut milk](/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Beef is a rarity,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but a popular dish is *[tinaktak](/wiki/Tinaktak "Tinaktak")*, ground beef in coconut milk.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Germany")]

Main article: [Christmas in Germany](/wiki/Christmas_in_Germany "Christmas in Germany")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Thronendes_Jesuskind_BNM.jpg/250px-Thronendes_Jesuskind_BNM.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thronendes_Jesuskind_BNM.jpg)

[Christkind](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind")

During the Christmas period, the [Weihnachtsmarkt](/wiki/Christmas_market "Christmas market") (Christmas market) becomes a feature of almost every city, town, or village in the German-speaking countries, where visitors enjoy stalls, entertainment, and savour food and *[Glühwein](/wiki/Mulled_wine "Mulled wine")* (mulled wine). Traditional Christmastime treats include [Lebkuchen](/wiki/Lebkuchen "Lebkuchen") (gingerbread), [Stollen](/wiki/Stollen "Stollen") (fruit cake), [Speculaas](/wiki/Speculaas "Speculaas") and [marzipan](/wiki/Marzipan "Marzipan") (almond confectionery often made into sweets). Perhaps the most famed of these markets is the [Christkindlesmarkt](/wiki/Christkindlesmarkt,_Nuremberg "Christkindlesmarkt, Nuremberg") held in [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"), which attracts millions of visitors every year.

The Weihnachtsbaum ([Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree")) is usually put up on the afternoon of 24 December. The trees can be bought at special traders' sites, but some families may still go into the forest and cut one themselves. The [Christkind](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind") brings the presents on the evening of Christmas eve.

Traditionally, on *Heiligabend* (Christmas Eve) in Germany, a simple meal will be prepared and served before or after the *Bescherung* ("time for exchanging gifts"), in contrast to the big meal on Christmas Day. Various polls repeatedly declare ([Eintopf](/wiki/Eintopf "Eintopf")) or sausages ([Würstchen](/wiki/Sausage#Germany "Sausage")) with potato salad to be Germany's favourite meal on *Heiligabend*. Further typical meals may include [carp](/wiki/Carp "Carp"), [fondue](/wiki/Fondue "Fondue") or [raclette](/wiki/Raclette "Raclette"). On Christmas Day, the most common dishes are [roast goose](/wiki/Roast_goose "Roast goose") or duck.

### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Italy")]

Main article: [Christmas in Italy](/wiki/Christmas_in_Italy "Christmas in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Feast_of_the_Seven_Fishes_11_%286517353129%29.jpg/250px-Feast_of_the_Seven_Fishes_11_%286517353129%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Feast_of_the_Seven_Fishes_11_(6517353129).jpg)

[Cannoli](/wiki/Cannoli "Cannoli") served at the [Feast of the Seven Fishes](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Seven_Fishes "Feast of the Seven Fishes")

[Christmas in Italy](/wiki/Christmas_in_Italy "Christmas in Italy") is one of the [country's major holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Italy "Public holidays in Italy") and begins on 8 December, with the [Feast of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Feast of the Immaculate Conception"), the day on which traditionally the [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree") is put up, and ends on 6 January of the following year with the [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)").[[24]](#cite_note-:2-24) According to tradition, the Christmas Eve dinner must not contain meat. It is quite common to attend [Midnight Mass](/wiki/Midnight_Mass "Midnight Mass") on Christmas Eve and practice the old custom of abstinence from meat on the day (but not fasting, which is observed by the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church")). Traditions regarding the exchanging of gifts vary from region to region, as this may take place either on Christmas Eve or on Christmas Day. On 24 December, Christmas Eve, the Christmas night mass, also called [Midnight Mass](/wiki/Midnight_Mass "Midnight Mass"), is celebrated.

While other Christian families throughout the world celebrate the Christmas Eve meal with various meats, Italians (especially Sicilians) celebrate the traditional [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") "[Feast of the Seven Fishes](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Seven_Fishes "Feast of the Seven Fishes")" which was historically served after a 24-hour fasting period. Although Christmas fasting is no longer a popular custom, some [Italian-Americans](/wiki/Italian-Americans "Italian-Americans") still enjoy a meatless Christmas Eve feast[[25]](#cite_note-RachaelRay-25) and attend the Midnight Mass. In various cultures, a festive dinner is traditionally served for the family and close friends in attendance, when the first star (usually [Sirius](/wiki/Sirius "Sirius")) appears in the sky.

### Lithuania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Lithuania")]

Further information: [Kūčios](/wiki/K%C5%AB%C4%8Dios "Kūčios")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/K%C5%AB%C4%8Dios.JPG/250px-K%C5%AB%C4%8Dios.JPG)](/wiki/File:K%C5%AB%C4%8Dios.JPG)

Lithuanian Christmas Eve table with *kūčiukai*

Lithuanian Christmas Eve blends pagan and Christian traditions, as initially it was a celebration of the winter solstice.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Traditionally, Lithuanians believed that animals could talk on that night, and it was possible to predict the future with charms and various games.[[27]](#cite_note-27) *[Kūčios](/wiki/K%C5%AB%C4%8Dios "Kūčios")* ("Holy Meal") is the most important event of the year and family reunion. Dead relatives are remembered with an empty plate set at the table.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The feast starts after the rise of the evening star.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] No products made from meat, milk and alcohol are allowed during the Kūčios.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] In all, 12 dishes are served, all of them rustic, made from grains, fish, dried fruit or mushrooms including *[kūčiukai](/wiki/K%C5%AB%C4%8Diukai "Kūčiukai")*. Small biscuits soaked in [poppy seed milk](/wiki/Poppy_milk "Poppy milk") are served.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After the dinner is over the table is left uncleared overnight for the feast of *[vėlės](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/v%C4%97l%C4%97 "wikt:vėlė")* (spirits or soul).[[31]](#cite_note-31)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*][[32]](#cite_note-32)

In [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), turkey and [panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone") are the stars of Christmas Eve.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Philippines")]

See also: [Christmas in the Philippines](/wiki/Christmas_in_the_Philippines "Christmas in the Philippines") and [Christmas dinner § Philippines](/wiki/Christmas_dinner#Philippines "Christmas dinner")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Traditional_Filipino_cuisine.jpg/250px-Traditional_Filipino_cuisine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional_Filipino_cuisine.jpg)

Typical traditional *Nochebuena* meal in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), with a [lechón](/wiki/Filipino_lechon "Filipino lechon") as the centerpiece

In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), the [traditional dinner](/wiki/Christmas_dinner#Philippines "Christmas dinner") (known as *nochebuena*) is served at midnight after the family attends the late evening [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") known as *[Misa de Gallo](/wiki/Misa_de_Gallo "Misa de Gallo")* (sometimes referred to as *[Misa de Aguinaldo](/wiki/Misa_de_Aguinaldo "Misa de Aguinaldo")*, "Gift Mass"). Common traditional dishes served for the main course include: [lechón](/wiki/Filipino_lechon "Filipino lechon"), various types of [pancit](/wiki/Pancit "Pancit") (noodles), [Filipino spaghetti](/wiki/Filipino_spaghetti "Filipino spaghetti"), *[hamonado](/wiki/Hamonado "Hamonado")*, *[jamón](/wiki/Christmas_ham "Christmas ham")*, *[queso de bola](/wiki/Edam_(cheese) "Edam (cheese)")*, *[morcón](/wiki/Morc%C3%B3n_(Filipino_cuisine) "Morcón (Filipino cuisine)")*, *[embutido](/wiki/Embutido_(Filipino_cuisine) "Embutido (Filipino cuisine)")*, [chicken galantina](/wiki/Chicken_galantina "Chicken galantina"), *[almondigas](/wiki/Almondigas "Almondigas")* (meatballs), *[paelya](/wiki/Paelya "Paelya")* (*[arroz valenciana](/wiki/Arroz_valenciana "Arroz valenciana")*, *[bringhe](/wiki/Bringhe "Bringhe")*, etc.), *[lumpia](/wiki/Lumpia "Lumpia")*, *[menudo](/wiki/Menudo_(stew) "Menudo (stew)")*, *[mechado](/wiki/Mechado "Mechado")*, *[caldereta](/wiki/Caldereta "Caldereta")*, *[callos](/wiki/Callos "Callos")*, [chicken pastel](/wiki/Chicken_pastel "Chicken pastel"), *[relyenong](/wiki/Relleno "Relleno") [bangús](/wiki/Bangus "Bangus")* (stuffed milkfish), *[lengua estofado](/wiki/Lengua_estofado "Lengua estofado")*, *[adobo](/wiki/Adobo_(Filipino_cuisine) "Adobo (Filipino cuisine)")*, and various types of barbecue (*[inihaw](/wiki/Inihaw "Inihaw")*). Almost all of these dishes are eaten with white rice. Desserts and side dishes include *[úbe halayá](/wiki/Ube_halaya "Ube halaya")*, [turon](/wiki/Turon_(food) "Turon (food)"), [leche flan](/wiki/Leche_flan "Leche flan"), [macaroni salad](/wiki/Macaroni_salad "Macaroni salad"), *[membrilyo](/wiki/Dulce_de_membrillo "Dulce de membrillo")*, [fruit salad](/wiki/Fruit_salad "Fruit salad"), [buko salad](/wiki/Buko_salad "Buko salad"), *[crema de fruta](/wiki/Crema_de_fruta "Crema de fruta")*, *[ensaymada](/wiki/Ensaymada "Ensaymada")*, [champorado](/wiki/Champorado "Champorado"), [mango float](/wiki/Mango_float "Mango float"), [fruitcake](/wiki/Fruitcake "Fruitcake"), *castañas* ([roasted chestnuts](/wiki/Roasted_chestnuts "Roasted chestnuts")), and various other *[kakanin](/wiki/Kakanin "Kakanin")* (rice cakes) like *[puto bumbong](/wiki/Puto_bumbong "Puto bumbong")*, *[bibingka](/wiki/Bibingka "Bibingka")*, *[suman](/wiki/Suman_(food) "Suman (food)")*, *[biko](/wiki/Biko_(food) "Biko (food)")*, and *[sapin-sapin](/wiki/Sapin-sapin "Sapin-sapin")*. Popular beverages are *[tsokolate](/wiki/Tsokolate "Tsokolate")* as well as coffee, [soda](/wiki/Soft_drinks "Soft drinks"), wine, beer, alcoholic drinks, and fruit juices.[[34]](#cite_note-Koppe-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Tan-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Poland")]

Main articles: [Wigilia](/wiki/Wigilia "Wigilia") and [Christmas in Poland](/wiki/Christmas_in_Poland "Christmas in Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Wigilia_potrawy_554.jpg/250px-Wigilia_potrawy_554.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wigilia_potrawy_554.jpg)

Traditional Polish *[Wigilia](/wiki/Wigilia "Wigilia")* meal

Christmas Eve is the most eventful day of the [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), and is characterized by the vigil supper called *[Wigilia](/wiki/Wigilia "Wigilia")* held across the country. All dishes served are meatless and their number is traditionally 12, but has been an odd number in the past.[[40]](#cite_note-40) According to the [Etymological Dictionary of the Polish Language](/wiki/S%C5%82ownik_etymologiczny_j%C4%99zyka_polskiego "Słownik etymologiczny języka polskiego") by [Aleksander Brückner](/wiki/Aleksander_Br%C3%BCckner "Aleksander Brückner"), the number of dishes was traditionally related to social class: the peasants' vigil consisted of 5 or 7 dishes, the [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry") usually served 9, and the [aristocracy](/wiki/Aristocracy "Aristocracy"), 11 dishes, but the even number 12 is also found today to remember the [Twelve Apostles](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles "Twelve Apostles"). It is obligatory to try a portion of all of them. Some traditions specify that the number of guests cannot be odd.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42). The Christmas Eve dinner has its roots in pagan Shchedry Vody, the winter solstice festival aimed at ensuring prosperity for the coming year. Sharing the wafer is a gesture of reconciliation and forgiveness that comes from Christian tradition. This custom appeared in Poland in the 18th century and quickly became one of the most moving moments of the holidays.[[43]](#cite_note-43).

[Don Q](/wiki/Don_Q "Don Q") or [Bacardi](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Rum "Cathedral of Rum") ([rum](/wiki/Rum "Rum")), and [Pitorro](/wiki/Pitorro "Pitorro") ([moonshine](/wiki/Moonshine "Moonshine") rum).[[46]](#cite_note-Navidadcomida2-46)

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Russia")]

*Rozhdenstvenskiy sochelnik* ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Рождественский сочельник) was a common Eastern Orthodox tradition in the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), but during the era of the Soviet Union it was greatly discouraged as a result of the official [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") of the former regime.

In modern-day Russia, [the church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") has a [service](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") on that day, but the celebration itself has not yet regained its popularity among the people. Instead of Christmas Eve, [New Year's Eve](/wiki/New_Year%27s_Eve "New Year's Eve") is considered to be a traditional family celebration featuring the [New Year tree](/wiki/New_Year_tree "New Year tree").

### Serbia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Serbia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Candle_lighting_a_plate_of_oranges_and_smarties_1.JPG/250px-Candle_lighting_a_plate_of_oranges_and_smarties_1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Candle_lighting_a_plate_of_oranges_and_smarties_1.JPG)

Candles on Christmas Eve 2010

In accordance with the [Christmas traditions of the Serbs](/wiki/Serbian_Christmas_traditions "Serbian Christmas traditions"), their festive meal has a copious and diverse selection of foods, although it is prepared according to the rules of fasting.

As well as a round, [unleavened](/wiki/Leavening_agent "Leavening agent") loaf of bread and salt, which are necessary, this meal may comprise roast fish, cooked beans, [sauerkraut](/wiki/Sauerkraut "Sauerkraut"), noodles with ground walnuts, honey, and wine.

Families in some Slavic countries leave an empty place at the table for guests (alluding to [Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus") and [Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph") looking for shelter in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem")).

### Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Ukraine")]

In Ukraine, *Sviatyi Vechir* ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Святий Вечір, Holy Evening) is traditionally celebrated with a meatless [twelve-dish Christmas Eve supper](/wiki/Twelve-dish_Christmas_Eve_supper "Twelve-dish Christmas Eve supper"), or the Holy Supper ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Свята Вечеря, Sviata Vecheria). The main attributes of the Holy Supper in Ukraine are [kutia](/wiki/Kutia "Kutia"), a poppy seed, honey and wheat dish, and [uzvar](/wiki/Uzvar "Uzvar"), a drink made from reconstituted dried fruits. Other typical dishes are [borscht](/wiki/Borscht "Borscht"), [varenyky](/wiki/Varenyky "Varenyky"), and dishes made of [fish](/wiki/Fish "Fish"), [phaseolus](/wiki/Phaseolus "Phaseolus") and [cabbage](/wiki/Cabbage "Cabbage").

The twelve dishes symbolize the [Twelve Apostles](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles "Twelve Apostles"). Just as in Poland, it is obligatory to try a portion of all of the dishes. The table is spread with a white cloth symbolic of the [swaddling clothes](/wiki/Swaddling_clothes "Swaddling clothes") the [Child Jesus](/wiki/Child_Jesus "Child Jesus") was wrapped in, and a large white candle stands in the center of the table symbolizing [Christ the Light of the World](/wiki/Light_of_the_World_(Jesus) "Light of the World (Jesus)"). Next to it is a round loaf of bread symbolizing Christ [Bread of Life](/wiki/Names_and_titles_of_Jesus_in_the_New_Testament#Bread_of_Life "Names and titles of Jesus in the New Testament"). [Hay](/wiki/Hay "Hay") is often displayed either on the table or as a decoration in the room, reminiscent of the [manger](/wiki/Manger "Manger") in [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem").

### Venezuela

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Venezuela")]

In [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"), [hallacas](/wiki/Hallaca "Hallaca") are normally the staple dish for Nochebuena alongside either ham or pork leg known as "pernil", [panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone"), [rum](/wiki/Rum "Rum") and "Ponche Crema" (a form of alcoholic [eggnog](/wiki/Eggnog "Eggnog")). The night is usually accompanied by traditional Christmas music known as "[aguinaldos](/wiki/Aguinaldo_(music) "Aguinaldo (music)")"; in Venezuela, the traditional music is known as [joropo](/wiki/Joropo "Joropo").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Gift giving

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Gift giving")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Gifts_xmas.jpg/250px-Gifts_xmas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gifts_xmas.jpg)

Christmas presents under the Christmas tree

In Christianity, the giving of gifts during Christmastide is traced to the [Magi](/wiki/Biblical_Magi "Biblical Magi") who brought gifts for the [Christ child](/wiki/Christ_child "Christ child") in the [manger](/wiki/Manger "Manger"). During the [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") in 16th- and 17th-century Europe, many Protestants changed the gift bringer to the Christ Child or *[Christkindl](/wiki/Christkindl "Christkindl")*, and the date of giving gifts changed from 6 December to Christmas Eve.[[48]](#cite_note-48) It is the night when [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus") makes his rounds delivering gifts to good children.

In Austria, Croatia, the Czech Republic, Hungary, and Slovakia, where [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas") (sv. Mikuláš/szent Mikulás) gives gifts on 6 December, the Christmas gift-giver is the [Child Jesus](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind") ([Ježíšek](/wiki/Je%C5%BE%C3%AD%C5%A1ek "Ježíšek") in [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"), Jézuska in Hungarian, Ježiško in [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language") and Isusek in [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language")).[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In Austria, the Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, Germany, Hungary, Norway, Poland, Slovakia, Sweden and Switzerland, presents are traditionally exchanged on the evening of 24 December. Children are commonly told that presents were brought either by the [Christkind](/wiki/Christkind "Christkind") (German for Christ child),[[50]](#cite_note-50) or by the [Weihnachtsmann](/wiki/Weihnachtsmann "Weihnachtsmann"). Both leave the gifts, but are in most families not seen doing so. In Germany, the gifts are also brought on 6 December by "the [Nikolaus](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas")" with his helper [Knecht Ruprecht](/wiki/Knecht_Ruprecht "Knecht Ruprecht").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Gezin_bij_de_kerstboom_c1860.jpg/250px-Gezin_bij_de_kerstboom_c1860.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gezin_bij_de_kerstboom_c1860.jpg)

Christmas tree with presents hanging on the tree

In Estonia *[Jõuluvana](/wiki/J%C3%B5uluvana "Jõuluvana")*, Finland *[Joulupukki](/wiki/Joulupukki "Joulupukki")*, Denmark *[Julemanden](/wiki/Julemanden "Julemanden")*, Norway *[Julenissen](/w/index.php?title=Julenissen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Julenissen (page does not exist)")* and Sweden *[Jultomten](/wiki/Jultomten "Jultomten")*, personally meets children and gives presents on the evening of Christmas Eve.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In Argentina, Austria, Brazil, Colombia, Croatia, the Czech Republic, Denmark, Estonia, the Faroe Islands, Finland, France, Germany, Hungary, Iceland, Latvia, Lithuania, Luxembourg, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Quebec (French Canada), Romania, Uruguay, Slovakia, Slovenia, Sweden, and Switzerland, Christmas presents are opened mostly on the evening of the 24th—following German tradition, this is also the practice among the [British royal family](/wiki/British_royal_family "British royal family") since it was introduced by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") and [Albert, Prince Consort](/wiki/Albert,_Prince_Consort "Albert, Prince Consort")[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)—while in Italy, the United States, the United Kingdom, Republic of Ireland, Malta, English Canada, South Africa, New Zealand and Australia, this occurs mostly on the morning of Christmas Day.

Some countries do their gift-giving on a different date, connected to the tradition either [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(feast) "Epiphany (feast)") (in Spain, the morning of 6 January)[[55]](#cite_note-55) or [Sinterklaas](/wiki/Sinterklaas "Sinterklaas") (Belgium and the Netherlands, on the evening of 5 December).[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Other traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Other traditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Flickr_-_The_U.S._Army_-_Christmas_Eve_Candlelight_Services.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_The_U.S._Army_-_Christmas_Eve_Candlelight_Services.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_The_U.S._Army_-_Christmas_Eve_Candlelight_Services.jpg)

A Christmas Eve candlelight [service](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") in [Baghdad](/wiki/Baghdad "Baghdad"), Iraq

Christmas Eve is celebrated in different ways around the world, varying by country and region. Elements common to many areas of the world include the attendance of special religious observances such as a midnight Mass or Vespers and the giving and receiving of presents. Along with Easter, Christmastime is one of the most important periods on the Christian calendar, and is often closely connected to other holidays at this time of year, such as Advent, the Feast of the Immaculate Conception, St. Nicholas Day, St. Stephen's Day, New Year's, and the Feast of the Epiphany.

Among Christians, as well as non-Christians who celebrate Christmas, the significant amount of vacation travel, and travel back to family homes, that takes place in the lead-up to Christmas means that Christmas Eve is also frequently a time of social events and parties, worldwide.[[57]](#cite_note-Fodor-57)[[58]](#cite_note-Discourse-58)[[59]](#cite_note-Culture-59)[[60]](#cite_note-Tonic-60)[[61]](#cite_note-Ohio-61)

Further information on Christmas Eve traditions around the world: [Christmas worldwide](/wiki/Christmas_worldwide "Christmas worldwide")

### In Jewish culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: In Jewish culture")]

[Nittel Nacht](/wiki/Nittel_Nacht "Nittel Nacht") is a name given to Christmas Eve by Jewish scholars in the 17th century.

#### In contemporary American-Jewish culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: In contemporary American-Jewish culture")]

With Christmas Day being a [work holiday](/wiki/Work_holiday "Work holiday") and Christmas Eve often being a partial work holiday throughout the United States, there is a space of unfilled free time during which much of American commerce and society is not functioning, and which can give rise to a sense of loneliness or alienation for American Jews.[[62]](#cite_note-Hwang-62)[[63]](#cite_note-AP2006-63)[[64]](#cite_note-Tracy-64)[[65]](#cite_note-Lewis-65)[[66]](#cite_note-ChinaDaily-66)

Jews also typically do not engage in the family gathering and religious worship activities that are central to Christmas Eve for Christians.[[67]](#cite_note-TimesDeciding-67)

Typical contemporary activities have usually been limited to "Chinese and a movie"[[68]](#cite_note-Karch-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Wiener-69)[[70]](#cite_note-Vozick-70)—consuming a meal at a [Chinese restaurant](/wiki/Chinese_restaurant "Chinese restaurant"), which tend to be open for business on the Christmas holiday, and watching a movie at the theater or at home, stereotypically a rerun of the 1946 film *[It's a Wonderful Life](/wiki/It%27s_a_Wonderful_Life "It's a Wonderful Life")*.[[66]](#cite_note-ChinaDaily-66)[[71]](#cite_note-Heyman2010-71)[[72]](#cite_note-Duckett-72)[[73]](#cite_note-Gluck-73)

Since the 1980s a variety of social events for young Jews have sprung up, and become popular, on Christmas Eve.[[74]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-74) These include the [Matzo Ball](/wiki/Matzo_Ball "Matzo Ball"), [The Ball](/wiki/The_Ball_(event) "The Ball (event)"), and a number of local events organized by Jewish communities and [local Jewish Federations](/wiki/Local_Jewish_Federations "Local Jewish Federations") in North America.[[63]](#cite_note-AP2006-63)

Further information on Christmas Eve social events for young Jews in North America: [Matzo Ball](/wiki/Matzo_Ball "Matzo Ball")

### In Chinese culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: In Chinese culture")]

In Mandarin, Christmas Eve is called *Píng'ān yè* (平安夜, "peaceful night", etymologically from the Chinese title of the Christmas carol *[Silent Night](/wiki/Silent_Night "Silent Night")*). People exchange apples, because the word for "apple" (苹(píng)果) is a rhyming wordplay with "peace" (平(píng)安).[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### In Inuit culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: In Inuit culture")]

In [Inuit](/wiki/Inuit "Inuit") territories, Christmas Eve is called *[Quviasukvik](/wiki/Quviasukvik "Quviasukvik")*. The Inuit celebrate it as their new year.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: United States")]

In [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") and areas of [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego,_CA "San Diego, CA"), California, Christmas Eve (*nochebuena*) is celebrated by lighting [luminarias](/wiki/Luminaria_(vigil_fire) "Luminaria (vigil fire)") and [farolitos](/wiki/Farolito "Farolito").

A cross, left near [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres") in Belgium in 1999, to commemorate the site of the 1914 Christmas Truce. The text reads *1914—The Khaki Chum's Christmas Truce—85 Years—Lest We Forget*.



The 1968 [Apollo 8](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8") Christmas Eve broadcast and reading from the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis")

A number of historical events have been influenced by the occurrence of Christmas Eve.

### Christmas truce

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Christmas truce")]

Main article: [Christmas truce](/wiki/Christmas_truce "Christmas truce")

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in 1914 and 1915 there was an unofficial [Christmas truce](/wiki/Christmas_truce "Christmas truce"), particularly between [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") and [German](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") troops. The truce began on Christmas Eve, 24 December 1914, when [German troops](/wiki/German_Army_(German_Empire) "German Army (German Empire)") began decorating the area around their [trenches](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") in the region of [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres"), Belgium, for Christmas. They began by placing candles on trees, then continued the celebration by singing [Christmas carols](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol"), most notably *[Stille Nacht](/wiki/Silent_Night_(song) "Silent Night (song)")* ("Silent Night"). The [British troops](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") in the trenches across from them responded by singing English carols. The two sides shouted Christmas greetings to each other. Soon there were calls for visits across the "[No man's land](/wiki/No_man%27s_land "No man's land")" when small gifts were exchanged. The truce also allowed a breathing space during which recently killed soldiers could be brought back behind their lines by burial parties. Funerals took place as soldiers from both sides mourned the dead together and paid their respects. At one funeral in No Man's Land, soldiers from both sides gathered and read a passage from [Psalm 23](/wiki/Psalm_23 "Psalm 23"). The truce occurred in spite of opposition at higher levels of the military command. Earlier in the autumn, a call by [Pope Benedict XV](/wiki/Pope_Benedict_XV "Pope Benedict XV") for an official truce between the warring governments had been ignored.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Scott_1371%2C_Apollo_8.jpg/250px-Scott_1371%2C_Apollo_8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scott_1371,_Apollo_8.jpg)

[Apollo 8](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8") commemorative stamp of the [Christmas Eve Genesis reading](/wiki/Apollo_8_Genesis_reading "Apollo 8 Genesis reading"), issued in 1969, includes the *[Earthrise](/wiki/Earthrise "Earthrise")* photograph by NASA astronaut [William Anders](/wiki/William_Anders "William Anders")

### Apollo 8 reading from Genesis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christmas_Eve&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Apollo 8 reading from Genesis")]

Main article: [Apollo 8 Genesis reading](/wiki/Apollo_8_Genesis_reading "Apollo 8 Genesis reading")

On 24 December 1968, in what was the most watched television broadcast to that date, the [Apollo 8](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8") astronauts [Bill Anders](/wiki/William_Anders "William Anders"), [Jim Lovell](/wiki/Jim_Lovell "Jim Lovell") and [Frank Borman](/wiki/Frank_Borman "Frank Borman") surprised the world with a reading of the Creation from the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis") as they orbited the Moon.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

In 1969, the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") issued a stamp ([Scott#](/wiki/Scott_number "Scott number") 1371) commemorating the [Apollo 8 flight around the Moon](/wiki/Apollo_8 "Apollo 8"). The stamp featured a detail of Anders' famous photograph *[Earthrise](/wiki/Earthrise "Earthrise")* of the Earth "rising" over the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") (NASA image AS8-14-2383HR), taken on Christmas Eve, and the words, "In the beginning [God](/wiki/God "God")...".
